title: Ezekiel 27
subtitle: Lament Over Tyre: The Merchant Ship

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Siege of Tyre

sections.header: Verses 1-25 — The Ship of Tyre and Its Cargo

heb.paragraph: The entire chapter personifies Tyre as a magnificent ship (oniyyah). The metaphor captures both Tyre’s grandeur and its vulnerability.
The trade catalogue in vv.12-25 is the most detailed inventory of ancient Near Eastern commerce in the Bible. Over 20 nations and their products are listed.

panels.ctx: Chapter 27 is a remarkable literary achievement: a funeral dirge for a city portrayed as a magnificent ship. The first section describes the ship’s construction from the finest materials of the known world, and the trade catalogue reads like a customs manifest of the ancient world.

cross.note: Solomon’s alliance with Hiram of Tyre for temple construction.
Revelation’s list of trade goods is modelled directly on Ezekiel 27.
Isaiah’s Tyre oracle also focuses on maritime commerce.

notes.note: The ship metaphor describes Tyre’s construction from the finest international materials.
Persia, Lydia, and Libya served as Tyre’s mercenary garrison.
The trade catalogue lists over 20 trading partners: Tarshish (silver, iron), Arabia (livestock), Judah and Israel (wheat, honey, oil).
Judah and Israel trade agricultural products: wheat, confections, honey, oil, and balm.
Calvin reads the ship as an emblem of human pride: every nation contributes, yet the whole structure is vulnerable.
Calvin: no nation is self-sufficient. True sufficiency comes only from God.
The geographic specificity is remarkable: fir from Senir, cedar from Lebanon, oaks from Bashan, cypress from Cyprus.
Tarshish is likely Tartessus in southern Spain, the western limit of Phoenician trading networks.
Block calls this 'the most important single source of information on ancient Near Eastern commerce in the Hebrew Bible.'
Zimmerli identifies two originally separate literary pieces — the ship allegory and the prose trade catalogue — combined into a single lament.

heb.paragraph: The nations 'hiss' (sharaq) at Tyre’s fall — an expression of horror and contempt. The same word describes the reaction to Jerusalem’s destruction in Jeremiah 19:8.

panels.ctx: The magnificent ship encounters the east wind and sinks in the heart of the seas. The drowning inverts Tyre’s maritime mastery: the sea that carried her wealth becomes her grave.

cross.note: God shatters the ships of Tarshish with an east wind.
The storm that wrecks Jonah’s ship uses similar imagery.
Revelation’s merchants 'stand far off' and weep directly echoing Tyre’s mourners.

notes.note: The east wind sinks the ship in the heart of the seas. Everything goes down.
The mourning rituals are extreme: dust, ashes, baldness, sackcloth, bitter weeping.
The final verdict: 'You have come to a horrible end and will be no more.'
Calvin identifies the east wind as divine providence: the very elements obey God’s decree.
The 'east wind' (ruach haq-qadim) is the hot, dry wind from the Arabian desert.
The mourning customs — casting dust, rolling in ashes, shaving the head — are well-attested ancient Near Eastern practices.
Block notes the devastating irony: the ship metaphor that celebrated Tyre’s maritime greatness now describes its maritime destruction.
Zimmerli reads the shipwreck as a cosmic event: the collapse of an entire world economic order.

themes.note: Sovereignty reaches its maximum as God sinks the ship that symbolised global trade.

lit.note: The allegory builds the magnificent ship piece by piece, loads it with the world’s wealth, then sinks it.

hebtext.note: The entire chapter personifies Tyre as a magnificent ship (oniyyah). The metaphor captures both Tyre’s grandeur and its vulnerability.

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Lament Over Tyre: The Merchant Ship

translations.text: Men of Sidon and Arvad were your oarsmen; your skilled men, Tyre, were aboard as your sailors.
The inhabitants of Sidon and Arvad were your rowers; your skilled men, O Tyre, were in you; they were your pilots.
Of oaks from Bashan they made your oars; of cypress wood from the coasts of Cyprus they made your deck, adorned with ivory.
Of oaks of Bashan they made your oars; they made your deck of pines from the coasts of Cyprus, inlaid with ivory.
